current_use: Government offices; historic museum; public visitor center; community event space
history_summary: Originally built in 1836 as the Jackson County Courthouse, the building served as the center of county government. It was extensively renovated in 1933 under Harry S. Truman, who preserved and emphasized its Colonial Revival style. Today it functions as a historic landmark, museum, and home to county offices.
original_use: County courthouse and seat of county government
sources: Jackson County Historical Society; National Register of Historic Places; Truman Library archives

pdf_content.overview:
The Jackson County Historic Truman Courthouse is located at 112 W Lexington Avenue in 
Independence, Missouri. It stands in the center of Independence Square, the historic downtown 
area of the city. Because of its location and history, the building represents the political, civic, and 
historical identity of Independence.
pdf_content.history_of_the_building:
The original courthouse was built in 1836. As Jackson County grew, 
the building needed more space and was expanded several times. Major 
renovations took place in 1848, 1873, 1887, and 1907. These changes affected 
the exterior design of the building, especially its clock tower. 
Later, in 1933, another important renovation took place while Harry 
S. Truman was serving as Presiding Judge of Jackson County. This 
renovation was designed by George Fredrick Wallace and it helped shape the 
courthouse’s current appearance and gave it many of its Colonial Revival 
features. 
Jackson County Courthouse ca 1900  
Connection to Harry S. Truman and Westward Expansion 
The courthouse is closely connected to Harry S. Truman, who later 
became the 33rd President of the United States. Before his presidency, 
Truman worked in Jackson County government, and this building became an 
important part of his early political career. Today, visitors can still see spaces 
related to Truman, including his historic office and courtroom. 
Jackson County Presiding Judge Harry S. 
Truman is speaking at the dedication of 
the Jackson County Courthouse in 
Independence, Missouri in 1933 
The courthouse is also connected to westward expansion 
because Independence was one of the most important starting 
points for migration to the western United States. Many travelers 
passed through the city before beginning long journeys on the 
Oregon Trail, Santa Fe Trail, and California Trail. Since the 
courthouse stood in the center of Independence Square, it was 
located near the heart of travel, trade, and government activity. 
 
The Santa Fe Trail Marker at the Jackson  
County Courthouse in Independence, Mo.
pdf_content.architecture:
The courthouse is mainly associated 
with the Colonial Revival style. This style uses 
classical and early American design elements 
to express order, tradition, and authority. 
Important 
architectural 
features 
include a balanced appearance, a central clock 
tower, brick construction, classical details, and 
a strong governmental image. These features 
help the building appear formal, stable, and 
important. 
The Jackson County Courthouse 2000 ca
pdf_content.original_and_current_use:
Originally, the courthouse was used for county government, administrative work, and 
judicial functions. It served as a central place for legal decisions and public services in Jackson 
County. 
Today, the building is preserved as a historic public site. It is used for tours, exhibits, 
archives, cultural interpretation, and some public services. As a result, the courthouse continues to 
serve the community while preserving its historical importance.
pdf_content.conclusion:
The Jackson County Historic Truman Courthouse is one of the most meaningful buildings in 
Independence, Missouri. Its history shows how the city developed as a center of government, travel, 
and national memory. Through its architecture and its connection to Truman and the western trails, 
the courthouse continues to represent the historic character of Independence Square.

architectural_style: Late 19th-Century Commercial / Brick Two-Part Commercial Block
current_use: Gift and floral boutique; modern retail shop
history_summary: Built in 1897, the two-story brick commercial building at 103 W. Lexington Avenue has served as a retail storefront for more than a century. Historic records show it housed businesses such as Fournie Saloon and Heckart Cigars (1910), People's Market and Clark's Studio (1920), Missouri Fruit Company (1930), The Leader general merchandise (1940), and Western Army Store (1960). The structure retains much of its appearance from the Truman era and is considered a contributing historic building within the Independence Square district. Today, it is occupied by Mixed Wildflower & Co., a boutique specializing in gifts, floral items, and decorative products, continuing the building’s long commercial tradition.
original_use: Commercial storefront serving various retail businesses
sources: Missouri Historic Resources Survey; Independence Square Historic District documentation; Commercial Records 1910–1960

architectural_style: Early 20th-Century Commercial / Brick Two-Part Commercial Block
current_use: Fitness and wellness facility; training center
history_summary: Constructed around 1920, the two-story brick commercial building at 109 W. Lexington Avenue has served a wide range of commercial and community functions on Independence Square. During the early 20th century, it operated as a specialty retail storefront and later became associated with the LTS and Sarsaparilla Dance Hall, reflecting the Square’s role as both a commercial and social hub. By 1950, the building housed a Singer Sewing Machine store, bringing a nationally recognized brand to downtown Independence. A major remodeling project in the 1980s altered the original storefront appearance, but the underlying historic brick structure remains intact and continues to contribute to the character of the Independence Square Historic District. Today, the building is occupied by Self-Propelled Fitness, a modern fitness and training facility that continues the property’s long tradition of commercial and community use.
original_use: Specialty retail storefront; commercial space
sources: Missouri Historic Resources Survey; Independence Square Historic District documentation; commercial records 1920–1980

pdf_content.conclusion:
The building at 109 W. Lexington Avenue, now occupied by Self-Propelled Fitness, is an 
important part of the historic Independence Square. Built around 1920, it has served a wide variety 
of commercial and community functions over time. The property has housed businesses ranging 
from retail stores and markets to social venues and nationally recognized brands such as Singer 
Sewing Machines. Although remodeled during the 1980s, the building continues to preserve its 
historic presence on the Square. Today, its use as a fitness facility continues more than a century of 
commercial and community activity while contributing to the preservation and vitality of downtown 
Independence.

architectural_style: Late 19th/Early 20th-Century Commercial / Brick Two-Part Commercial Block
current_use: Music store specializing in instruments and equipment
history_summary: The two-story brick commercial block at 121–123 W. Lexington Avenue was constructed in the late 1890s or early 1900s during a period of major commercial growth on Independence Square. The building originally featured dual storefronts, a central stairway to upper-floor offices, large display windows, terra-cotta trim, a metal cornice, and cast-iron lintels that survive today. It is historically associated with long-running pharmacies, most notably the Yantis-Fritts Drugstore (1914–1944), followed by office space in 1950 and Bunyar Drug Store in 1960. Although some decorative parapet elements were removed after 1950, the structure remains a contributing historic building within the Independence Square Historic District. Today, it houses Music Gear, a local music store that continues the building’s long commercial legacy while supporting the vitality of downtown Independence.
original_use: Two-part commercial storefront with retail and upper-floor office space
sources: Missouri Historic Resources Survey; Independence Square Historic District documentation; commercial records 1890–1960

current_use: Restaurant and tavern
history_summary: Constructed around 1909, the two-story commercial block at 113–115 W. Lexington Avenue reflects the modernization of Independence Square during the early twentieth century. Built with prominent storefronts facing the Jackson County Courthouse and a limestone-walled basement beneath the sidewalk, the structure quickly became a central part of the Square’s commercial and social life. The Courthouse Exchange business traces its origins to 1899, making it one of the oldest continuously operating establishments on the Square. Over the decades, the basement tavern evolved into a well-known gathering place that survived periods of economic decline and remained active through the Truman era and beyond. Today, Courthouse Exchange continues to operate as a restaurant and tavern, preserving its historic atmosphere while contributing to the vitality of downtown Independence.
original_use: Commercial storefront with basement tavern space
sources: Missouri Historic Resources Survey; Independence Square Historic District documentation; Courthouse Exchange historical records 1899–present

current_use: Board game café; community gaming and event space
history_summary: Originally constructed around 1910 as a Mission Revival-style commercial building, the structure at 107 W. Lexington Avenue once featured an elaborate shaped parapet and decorative façade elements. During the mid-twentieth century, the parapet was removed and the upper façade was covered with corrugated metal siding, significantly altering its appearance. The building is notable for its unusual design, which included both a street-level entrance and a below-grade storefront accessed by stairs, allowing for multiple tenants and specialized retail uses. Throughout the twentieth century, the building housed a wide variety of businesses, including shoe stores, cigar shops, restaurants, barber shops, beauty parlors, and sewing machine retailers. These businesses served courthouse employees, shoppers, and local residents, making the building a longstanding center of commercial and social activity on Independence Square. Today, Game Café occupies the space, continuing the building’s tradition as a community gathering place through board games, tournaments, and social events.
original_use: Mission Revival-style commercial storefront with dual-level retail space
sources: Missouri Historic Resources Survey; Independence Square Historic District documentation; commercial records 1910–1960

pdf_content.overview: Game Cafe – 107 W. Lexington Avenue
pdf_content.basic_information:
Game Cafe is located at 107 W. Lexington Avenue on the 
historic Independence Square in Independence, Missouri. Today, 
it serves as a community gathering place focused on board 
games, role-playing games, tournaments, and social events. The 
building has a long commercial history that reflects the changing

Game Café
pdf_content.building_description_and_construction_da:
According to the Missouri Historic Resources Survey, the building was originally 
constructed as a Mission Revival-style commercial building. When first built, it featured an 
elaborate shaped parapet above the roofline that gave the structure a much more decorative 
appearance than it has today. 
 
During the mid-twentieth century, the original parapet was removed and the upper façade 
was covered with corrugated metal siding, significantly altering the building's appearance. 
 
One of the building's most distinctive architectural features was the inclusion of both a 
street-level entrance and a below-grade storefront accessed by stairs. This unusual design allowed 
for additional commercial space and suggests the building may have been intended for multiple 
tenants or specialized retail uses from the beginning.
pdf_content.historical_appearance:
The building played an important role in the commercial life of Independence Square 
throughout the twentieth century. Historic photographs taken during and after the Truman 
administration show the building as part of the streetscape that became nationally recognized as the 
hometown of President Harry S. Truman. 
 
Although the façade has been altered over time, traces of its commercial history remain 
visible. Historic signage from former businesses can still be seen on the building, providing a direct 
connection to its past occupants.
pdf_content.businesses_over_time:
Throughout its history, the building housed multiple businesses simultaneously, including 
restaurants, barber shops, beauty parlors, cigar stores, and specialty retailers.

The Cigar and Restaurant Era 
 
During the early twentieth century, the building housed both retail and food-service 
businesses. Shoe stores, cigar shops, restaurants, and lunch counters operated from the location, 
serving shoppers, travelers, courthouse employees, and local residents visiting Independence 
Square. 
 
The combination of dining and retail businesses reflects the Square's importance as a 
regional commercial center during this period. 
 
The Barber Shop Decades 
 
From the 1930s until the 1950s, barber shops and beauty parlors became the tenants of the building. 
 
The repeated appearance of Cafe Deluxe Barber 
Shop under different owners suggests that it was a 
long-running and well-known establishment. During this 
era, barber shops served not only as places for grooming but 
also as important community gathering spaces where 
residents discussed local politics, business, sports, and 
courthouse news. Because of its location near county 
government offices, the business likely attracted attorneys, 
judges, merchants, and public officials as regular customers.

The café serves as a gathering place for board games,

tradition of social interaction that has characterized the

pdf_content.conclusion:
The building at 107 W. Lexington Avenue, now occupied by Game Café, is an important part 
of the historic Independence Square. Originally constructed as a Mission Revival-style commercial 
building, it has housed a wide variety of businesses over time, including shoe stores, cigar shops, 
restaurants, barber shops, beauty parlors, and sewing machine retailers. Today, Game Café 
continues the building's long tradition as a place where people gather, socialize, and build 
community while helping preserve the historic character of downtown Independence.

architectural_style: Late Victorian Commercial / Brick Two-Part Commercial Block
current_use: Vacant / repurposed commercial space (taxidermy business relocated)
history_summary: Constructed between 1895 and 1898, the Owens–Wyatt Building is one of the oldest surviving commercial structures on Independence Square. Designed as a two-story brick commercial block with two storefronts and a central stairway to upper-floor offices, the building retains significant original features including a pressed-metal cornice, terra-cotta detailing, cast-iron lintels, and large upper-story windows. The storefront at 123 W. Lexington Avenue housed numerous businesses throughout the twentieth century, including Model Ladies Furnishings (1920), Tadlock Tire Company (1940), Moore’s Fashion Shop (1950), and Slusher’s Shoe Shop (1960), whose owner later became mayor of Independence. In recent decades, the space was home to Craig Jones Taxidermy before the business relocated. Today, the building remains a well-preserved example of late nineteenth-century commercial architecture and continues to contribute to the historic character of Independence Square.
original_use: Late 19th-century commercial storefront within a two-part commercial block
sources: Missouri Historic Resources Survey; Independence Square Historic District documentation; commercial records 1895–present

pdf_content.building_description_and_construction_da:
The Owens–Wyatt Building was constructed between approximately 1895 and 1898 during a 
period of significant commercial growth in Independence. The structure was designed as a 
two-story brick commercial block containing two storefronts, now known as 121 and 123 W. 
Lexington Avenue, with a central entrance leading to offices and commercial space on the second 
floor. Historic surveys note that the building retains many original architectural features, including: 
●​
Decorative pressed-metal cornice 
●​
Terra-cotta detailing near the roofline 
●​
Cast-iron storefront lintels 
●​
Large upper-story windows 
●​
Original commercial storefront proportions 
●​
Central stairway to the second floor 
Although some decorative parapet elements were removed during the twentieth century, the 
building remains one of the best-preserved historic commercial blocks on Independence Square.
pdf_content.historical_appearance:
The Owens–Wyatt Building retains much of its original late-Victorian commercial character. 
Unlike many buildings on the Square that were heavily altered or replaced, this structure continues 
to reflect the appearance it would have had during the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. 
 

 
 
 
Its preservation allows modern visitors to experience a building that would still be 
recognizable to shoppers, merchants, and residents who visited Independence Square more than 125 
years ago. 
The People Behind the Name 
The building's historic name, Owens–Wyatt Building, suggests that it was developed or 
owned by individuals named Owens and Wyatt. Naming commercial buildings after owners or 
investors was common practice during the late nineteenth century and reflected the importance of 
local business leaders in the development of downtown Independence.
pdf_content.businesses_over_time:
The storefront at 123 W. Lexington Avenue housed a variety of businesses throughout the 
twentieth century, reflecting changing consumer needs and economic trends.

This timeline illustrates the transition from apparel retail to automotive services, back to 
fashion retail, shoe repair, and eventually specialty taxidermy services. 
Notable Businesses 
Model Ladies Furnishings (1920–1930) 
For at least a decade, the storefront housed Model Ladies Furnishings, a women's apparel 
store that served shoppers during the years between World War I and the Great Depression. 
Tadlock Tire Company (1940) 
By 1940, the storefront was occupied by Tadlock Tire Company, an automobile-related 
business reflecting the growing importance of automobiles and road transportation in American life. 
Moore's Fashion Shop (1950) 
 

 
 
 
Following World War II, the building returned to apparel sales as Moore's Fashion Shop 
during a period when Independence was gaining national attention as of President Harry S. Truman. 
Slusher's Shoe Shop (1960) 
By 1960, the storefront housed Slusher's Shoe Shop. The proprietor, Donald Slusher became 
mayor of Independence, giving the building a direct connection to the city's political history. 
Current Significance 
Although Craig Jones Taxidermy has moved to a different location, the building continues to 
represent the long commercial history of Independence Square. The storefront remains an example 
of how historic buildings can adapt to changing businesses while preserving their architectural and 
historical significance.
pdf_content.conclusion:
The building at 123 W. Lexington Avenue, part of the historic Owens–Wyatt Building, is one 
of the oldest surviving commercial structures on Independence Square. Constructed in the late 
nineteenth century, it has housed a wide variety of businesses including clothing stores, a tire 
company, a shoe shop, and Craig Jones Taxidermy. Its strong architectural integrity and connection 
to the Truman era make it an important contributor to the historic character of downtown 
Independence.

year_built: Unkown / around 1940
current_use: Specialty retail shop; community gathering space
history_summary: Constructed around 1940, the Gentry Building at 205–207 W. Lexington Avenue represents a major shift in the architectural character of Independence Square. Unlike the surrounding Victorian-era buildings with ornate brickwork and decorative cornices, this structure embraced the Moderne style, featuring smooth surfaces, horizontal lines, minimal ornamentation, large display windows, and reinforced concrete construction. Identified by the Missouri Historic Resources Survey as one of the best examples of Modern commercial architecture on the Square, the building symbolized progress and modernization when it was constructed. Over the decades, it housed businesses such as Tucker Furniture Store and Turner Music before becoming home to Be Here Now, a specialty retail business and community space. Today, the building continues its long tradition of commercial use while contributing to the architectural diversity and vitality of downtown Independence.
original_use: Modern commercial storefront built in the Moderne architectural style
sources: Missouri Historic Resources Survey; Independence Square Historic District documentation; commercial records 1940–present

historical_name: Jackson County Jail and Marshal’s Home – 217 N. Main Street
architectural_style: Mid-19th-Century Institutional / Federal-style with limestone jail block
current_use: Historic museum interpreting law enforcement and Civil War history
history_summary: Constructed in 1859, the Jackson County Jail and Marshal’s Home served as the county jail for 74 years, combining a family residence in the front portion with a limestone jail block behind it. The building played a major role during the Civil War, functioning as the U.S. Provost Marshal’s office and holding both civilian and military prisoners, including Confederate guerrilla leader William Clarke Quantrill. It later housed prisoners during the Prohibition era and the Great Depression. After being decommissioned in 1933, the structure was preserved and restored by the Jackson County Historical Society, opening as a museum in 1959. Today, the 1859 Jail Museum allows visitors to explore original jail cells, the marshal’s home, and the cell where Frank James was held, preserving one of the oldest surviving buildings in Jackson County and a key site in Independence’s legal and Civil War history.
original_use: County jail and marshal’s residence
sources: Jackson County Historical Society; Independence Square Historic District documentation; Civil War prisoner records

pdf_content.overview: 1859 Jail Museum - 217 N. Main Street
pdf_content.basic_information:
The 1859 Jail Museum, originally known as the Jackson County Jail 
and Marshal’s Home, is located at 217 N. Main Street on the historic 
Independence Square in Independence, Missouri. The building was 
constructed in 1859 and served as the third Jackson County jail. 
Today, the site is preserved as a museum by the Jackson County 
Historical Society. Visitors can tour the old jail, the marshal’s home, 
the marshal’s office, and the cell where Frank James was held. 
1859 jail Museum
pdf_content.building_description_and_construction_da:
The jail was built in 1859 as both a correctional facility and a residence. The front 
portion served as the home for the jailer, marshal, and their families, while the jail cells 
were located behind the residence. 
According to the Jackson County Historical Society, visitors can still see the 
limestone jail area and the heavy iron doors that guarded the cells. The building is also 
described as one of the oldest surviving structures in Jackson County. 
Original Use 
The building served as the Jackson County jail for 74 years, from 1859 until 1933. It 
held people accused or convicted of different crimes, and it also functioned as a residence 
for the jailer and later county marshals. 
This combination of domestic life and imprisonment makes the building especially 
interesting. Families lived only a short distance away from the prisoners, which shows how 
different nineteenth-century jail operations were from modern correctional facilities. 
Civil War Period 
 

 
 
 
During the American Civil War, the jail played an important role in the 
Missouri-Kansas border conflict. It was used to hold both military and civilian prisoners 
and also functioned as the U.S. Provost Marshal’s office. 
One of the most notable Civil War prisoners was 
William Clarke Quantrill, a Confederate guerrilla leader. 
He was reportedly held there after refusing to pledge 
loyalty to the Union. 
The jail’s Civil War history is important because it 
connects the building to the violence and political tension 
of Missouri during the war. Independence and Jackson 
County were deeply affected by divided loyalties, 
guerrilla warfare, and military control.​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
​
Inside 1859 Jail
pdf_content.preservation_and_current_use:
The jail was also used during later periods of local history, including the Prohibition 
era and the Great Depression. Over time, the building reflected changing ideas about 
crime, punishment, public safety, and county government. 
After the jail was decommissioned in 1933, it was eventually preserved rather than 
demolished. The Jackson County Historical Society helped restore and convert the building 
into a museum, which opened during the jail’s centennial year in 1959. 
Today, the 1859 Jail Museum allows visitors to learn about frontier justice, Civil War 
conflict, local law enforcement, and the lives of both prisoners and jailers. The museum 
preserves the jail cells, marshal’s home, and historic spaces connected to Jackson County’s 
legal history.
pdf_content.conclusion:
The 1859 Jail Museum at 217 N. Main Street is one of the most important historic 
buildings on Independence Square. Built in 1859, it served as the Jackson County jail for 74 
years before being decommissioned in 1933. Its history includes Civil War prisoners, the 
U.S. Provost Marshal’s office, the famous imprisonment of Frank James, and the daily lives 
 

 
 
 
of jailers and their families. Today, the museum preserves these stories and helps explain 
the history of law enforcement, crime, and justice in Independence, Missouri. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Clinton’s Soda Fountain - 100 W. Maple Avenue

year_built: Unknown / late nineteenth century
current_use: Historic-style soda fountain and visitor attraction
history_summary: Constructed in the late nineteenth century during a period of major commercial growth on Independence Square, the building at 100 W. Maple Avenue became home to Clinton’s Drugstore by the 1890s. The drugstore served as a retail center and community gathering place, offering medicines, stationery, books, tobacco, cosmetics, and refreshments. In 1898, fourteen-year-old Harry S. Truman worked here at his first paying job, sweeping floors, stocking shelves, assisting customers, and helping at the soda fountain. This early experience became one of the most meaningful memories of Truman’s youth. Although downtown business patterns changed during the twentieth century, the building survived and was later restored as Clinton’s Soda Fountain, recreating the atmosphere of an early twentieth-century soda shop. A statue of young Truman sweeping outside the building commemorates his first job and preserves the site’s connection to the future president’s early life.
original_use: Late 19th-century drugstore and soda fountain
sources: Truman Library historical notes; Independence Square Historic District documentation; Clinton’s Soda Fountain historical materials

pdf_content.overview:
Clinton’s Soda Fountain is located at 100 W. Maple Avenue, on the northwest corner of 
Main Street and Maple Avenue at Independence Square in Independence, Missouri. The building is 
one of the most historically important commercial sites on 
the Square because of its connection to Harry S. Truman, 
who later became the 33rd President of the United States. 
Today, the building operates as Clinton’s Soda 
Fountain, a business inspired by the original drugstore and 
soda fountain that once occupied the site. 
Clinton’ s Soda Fountain
pdf_content.construction_and_early_history:
The building was constructed during the late nineteenth century, when Independence 
Square was experiencing major commercial growth. After the Civil War, Independence developed as 
an important county seat and business center. During this period, many earlier frame buildings 
around the Square were replaced by more permanent brick commercial structures. 
Because the building occupied a prominent corner lot, it became a valuable location for 
businesses serving residents, travelers, farmers, and courthouse visitors. By the 1890s, the building 
housed Clinton’s Drugstore, operated by pharmacist J. H. Clinton. 
Drugstores during this period were more than places to buy medicine. They also sold items 
such as stationery, books, tobacco products, cosmetics, and refreshments. Many included soda 
fountains, which made them popular gathering places in local communities. 
Harry Truman’s First Job 
The building’s greatest historical significance comes from its connection to Harry S. 
Truman. In 1898, when Truman was fourteen years old, he got his first paying job at Clinton’s 
Drugstore. The Truman family lived nearby, so young Truman worked at the store before and after 
school. 
His duties included sweeping and cleaning floors, dusting merchandise, running errands, 
assisting customers, stocking shelves, helping at the soda fountain, and opening the store in the 
mornings. 
Truman later remembered earning three silver dollars for his first week of work. After 
receiving the money, he wanted to give it to his father, but his father told him to keep it. This 
 

 
 
 
experience stayed with Truman because it taught him responsibility, punctuality, customer service, 
and basic business skills. 
For this reason, Clinton’s Drugstore became one of the most meaningful places connected to 
Truman’s youth. 
Twentieth-Century Changes 
Like many commercial buildings on Independence Square, the structure changed over time 
as downtown business patterns shifted. After World War II, suburban shopping centers became more 
popular, and many traditional downtown districts experienced decline. 
However, the former Clinton Drugstore building survived. Its preservation allowed 
Independence to maintain a direct connection to both Truman’s early life and the history of the 
city’s downtown commercial district.
pdf_content.restoration_and_current_use:
Today, the building operates as Clinton’s Soda 
Fountain. Its modern use reflects the history of the original 
drugstore and soda fountain that once occupied the site. 
The interior is designed to evoke the atmosphere of 
an early twentieth-century soda fountain. This allows 
visitors to experience a historic-style business while 
learning about Truman’s connection to the location. 
       Harry’s First Job Memorial Statue 
Because of its association with Truman, the building is frequently included in tours of the 
historic Truman district and is recognized as one of the most important commercial buildings 
connected to his youth. 
Outside the building, visitors can also see a statue of 
young Harry S. Truman sweeping the floor. This statue 
represents Truman’s first job and helps connect the 
present-day site to his childhood experience. 
Harry’s First Job Memorial Statue
pdf_content.conclusion:
The building at 100 W. Maple Avenue, now known as Clinton’s Soda Fountain, is one of the 
most meaningful commercial buildings on Independence Square. Built during the late nineteenth 
century, it became home to Clinton’s Drugstore by the 1890s. In 1898, fourteen-year-old Harry S. 
Truman worked there at his first paying job, performing tasks such as sweeping floors, stocking 
merchandise, helping customers, and working at the soda fountain. Today, the restored soda 
fountain and the statue of young Truman preserve this important connection between 
Independence’s local business history and the early life of a future president.

history_summary: Constructed in the mid-1970s and opened on June 20, 1975 as the Independence Square Cinema, the building at 114 W. Maple Avenue was designed as a four-screen theater operated by Mid-America Theatres of St. Louis. Its construction reflected a major shift in the movie industry, as multi-screen cinemas were replacing older single-screen theaters. Built directly on Independence Square, the theater added a modern entertainment use to a district known for government, shopping, dining, and historic tourism. pdf_content.basic_information:

historic Independence Square in Independence, Missouri. Unlike

Pharaoh 4 Cinema
pdf_content.construction_and_opening:
The theater was constructed in the mid-1970s and officially opened on June 20, 1975 as the 
Independence Square Cinema. At the time, it was operated by Mid-America Theatres of St. Louis. 
The building was designed as a four-screen cinema, which reflected an important change in 
the movie industry. By the 1970s, multi-screen cinemas were becoming more common and were 
gradually replacing many older single-screen theaters. 
Because the theater was built directly on Independence Square, it added a modern 
entertainment use to a district already known for government, shopping, restaurants, and historic 
tourism. 
Transition to Pharaoh 4 Cinema 
Over time, the theater experienced changes in ownership and 
management. Around 2008, it was renamed Pharaoh 4 Cinema. The new 
name gave the theater a more distinctive identity while keeping its 
connection to Independence Square. 
 

 
 
 
The theater’s survival is important because many smaller neighborhood cinemas closed as 
large multiplex chains expanded. Pharaoh 4 Cinema remained active by continuing to serve the local 
community and by adapting its programming to modern audiences. 
​

Modern Use 
Today, Pharaoh 4 Cinema continues to operate as a four-screen movie theater. It shows 
current movies and also offers community-oriented events. 
The theater’s website lists programs such as Retro Movie Nights, Cinema Night Classics, 
Moonlight Movies, auditorium rentals, advertising opportunities, and an events calendar. These 
programs show that the theater is not only a place to watch new films, but also a community 
entertainment space. 
During the summer, visitors can attend outdoor movie events such as Moonlight Movies, 
which are shown in the Pharaoh theater parking lot. The theater has also offered classic movie 
events, including films projected directly onto the building. These activities help bring families and 
visitors to Independence Square outside of regular movie showtimes.
pdf_content.conclusion:
The building at 114 W. Maple Avenue, now home to Pharaoh 4 Cinema, is one of the most 
distinctive buildings on Independence Square. Opened on June 20, 1975 as the Independence Square 
Cinema, it was built as a four-screen theater during a period when multi-screen cinemas were 
becoming more popular. Later renamed Pharaoh 4 Cinema, around 2008, the theater has continued 
to serve the Independence community through regular movie showings, classic film events, and 
summer programs such as Moonlight Movies. Its long operation as a cinema makes it an important 
part of the Square’s entertainment history and modern community life.

pdf_content.overview:
Gilbert Whitney & Co. 
The building at 104 N. Liberty Street occupies a prominent location just north of 
Independence Square in one of the most historically significant commercial districts in 
Missouri. Today it is home to Gilbert, Whitney & Co., a boutique specializing in gifts, home 
décor, seasonal merchandise, vintage-inspired furnishings, candles, and specialty retail 
items. 
Although the current business represents the building's modern chapter, the structure itself is 
part of the historic commercial fabric that has defined downtown Independence for more 
than a century. 
Liberty Street and the Growth of Independence 
Liberty Street developed as one of the principal commercial corridors feeding into 
Independence Square, the governmental and economic center of Jackson County. During 
the late nineteenth century, merchants, professionals, and retailers established businesses 
along Liberty Street to take advantage of the steady flow of courthouse visitors, shoppers, 
and travelers. 
By the late 1800s and early 1900s, the area surrounding 104 N. Liberty Street was occupied 
by: 
●​ Retail storefronts 
●​ Professional offices 
●​ Dry goods merchants 
●​ Specialty shops 
●​ Service businesses 
The street quickly became an important extension of the Square's commercial activity and 
remains an active business district today. 
Architectural Character 
The building reflects the traditional commercial architecture that became common around 
Independence Square during the late nineteenth century. Its form is consistent with the 
narrow storefront buildings that were designed to maximize street frontage while 
accommodating a variety of commercial uses. 
 

 
 
 
Features typical of buildings from this period include: 
●​ Ground-floor retail space 
●​ Large storefront display windows 
●​ Shared walls with neighboring businesses 
●​ Flexible interior layouts that could adapt to changing tenants 
This adaptability helped many Independence Square buildings survive through changing 
economic conditions and evolving business needs. 
Connection to Independence History 
Because of its location within the historic core of downtown Independence, the building has 
witnessed many important chapters in the city's development. 
During its lifetime, the structure has stood through: 
●​ Independence's growth as a regional trade center 
●​ The arrival of electric lighting and telephones 
●​ The transition from horse-drawn transportation to automobiles 
●​ The Great Depression 
●​ World War II 
●​ The postwar suburban era 
●​ The revitalization of Independence Square 
The building is also located within walking distance of sites associated with Harry S. 
Truman. Throughout Truman's life, Independence Square remained the center of civic and 
commercial activity, and buildings along Liberty Street formed part of the streetscape he 
would have known well. 
Gilbert, Whitney & Co. 
Today, Gilbert, Whitney & Co. continues the building's long tradition of serving the public 
through locally owned commerce. 
The boutique offers: 
●​ Home décor 
●​ Gifts 
●​ Seasonal merchandise 
●​ Candles 
●​ Vintage-inspired furnishings 
●​ Specialty retail items 
 

 
 
 
Its presence reflects the ongoing revitalization of Independence Square, where historic 
buildings continue to find new life through independent businesses that attract both residents 
and visitors. 
Historical Significance 
The significance of 104 N. Liberty Street lies in its contribution to the historic character and 
continuity of downtown Independence. For generations, buildings like this one have formed 
the backbone of the Square's commercial district, providing space for local entrepreneurship 
and community life. 
More than a century after its construction, the building continues to fulfill the purpose for 
which it was created: serving the public through commerce while preserving the architectural 
and historical character that makes Independence Square one of Missouri's most treasured 
historic districts. 
 
Independence Square Association 
The building at 106 S. Liberty Street is home to the Independence Square Association 
(ISA), the nonprofit organization dedicated to promoting, preserving, and revitalizing 
Independence Square. Today, the building serves as the administrative headquarters for one 
of the most influential organizations in downtown Independence. 
The Building and Its Setting 
Located just south of Independence Square, 106 S. Liberty Street sits within the heart of the 
city's historic commercial district. The building is part of the collection of storefronts and 
commercial structures that have supported business, civic activity, and community life 
around the Square for generations. 
Like many buildings in the district, it contributes to the historic streetscape that makes 
Independence Square one of Missouri's most recognizable and well-preserved downtowns. 
Independence Square Association

The most significant documented occupant of the building is the Independence Square 
Association. 
Founded to support the preservation and economic vitality of Independence Square, the 
organization works closely with local businesses, property owners, community groups, and 
the City of Independence. 
The Association's activities include: 
●​ Organizing community events 
●​ Supporting local businesses 
●​ Promoting tourism 
●​ Encouraging historic preservation 
●​ Coordinating downtown improvement projects 
●​ Marketing Independence Square as a destination 
Many of the festivals, seasonal celebrations, and promotional events held on the Square are 
coordinated or supported through the Association. 
Historical Context 
Because of its location within the historic district, the building has witnessed many important 
periods in Independence's development, including: 
●​ The growth of Independence as Jackson County's commercial center 
●​ The transition from horse-drawn transportation to automobiles 
●​ The Great Depression 
●​ World War II 
●​ Twentieth-century commercial expansion 
●​ Downtown decline during the suburban shopping era 
●​ Modern preservation and revitalization efforts 
Its location places it at the center of Independence's continuing story.

Historical Significance 
 

 
 
 
The significance of 106 S. Liberty Street comes not from a famous retailer or nationally 
known business, but from its role as the headquarters of the organization dedicated to 
preserving and strengthening Independence Square itself. 
In many ways, the building serves as the operational center of downtown Independence's 
preservation movement. While surrounding buildings house restaurants, shops, galleries, 
and attractions, 106 S. Liberty Street is where much of the planning, coordination, and 
advocacy takes place to ensure that Independence Square remains a vibrant and historically 
significant destination for future generations. 
 
Mixed Mutt & Company 
The building at 108 S. Liberty Street is a historic commercial storefront located just south of 
Independence Square. Today it is occupied by Mixed Mutt & Co, a pet boutique and artisan 
dog ice cream shop that has become one of the more unique businesses on the Square.  
The company's most documented chapter began when it established its Independence 
Square location. 
The business specializes in: 
●​ Pet toys and accessories 
●​ Dog treats 
●​ Collars and bandanas 
●​ Gifts for pet owners 
●​ Artisan dog ice cream through its "Mixed Mutt Creamery" brand 
The company emphasizes sustainable and eco-friendly products and frequently partners 
with women-owned businesses. The Mixed Mutt & Co. is a participant in the ongoing 
revitalization of Independence Square, from the early 2020s to the present. 
What We Still Know About the Building 
While a complete list of earlier tenants has not surfaced in digitized records, the building's 
significance lies in its continuity as a commercial storefront within the historic district. It 
remains an example of how Independence Square's historic buildings continue to adapt to 
modern businesses while preserving the character of downtown.

pdf_content.overview:
Historical Significance 
108 S. Liberty Street represents the ongoing evolution of Independence Square. Originally 
built as part of the city's historic commercial district, the building continues to serve the public 
through locally owned business. Today, through Mixed Mutt & Co, it combines retail, 
community events, and pet-friendly activities, adding a unique chapter to the long 
commercial history of Independence Square. 
 
Designers Avenue Upholstery 
The building at 202 N. Liberty Street is located just north of Independence Square and is 
currently occupied by Designers Avenue Upholstery, one of the longest-established specialty 
upholstery businesses in downtown Independence. The structure forms part of the historic 
commercial district that has supported local businesses for generations. Designers Avenue 
Upholstery does restoration of family heirlooms and antique furniture. Designers Avenue 
Upholstery has been in business from 2006 to the present day. 
What Makes This Building Interesting 
Unlike many storefronts on Independence Square that house restaurants, boutiques, or 
entertainment venues, 202 N. Liberty Street has become known for a traditional skilled trade. 
Upholstery shops were once common features of American downtowns, but relatively few 
remain today. 
As a result, the building continues a craft-based commercial tradition that connects modern 
Independence with earlier generations of furniture makers, repair specialists, and artisans. 
Historical Significance 
The significance of 202 N. Liberty Street lies not only in the age of the building but also in the 
type of business it houses.

For nearly two decades—and possibly longer—the storefront has been associated with the 
preservation and restoration of furniture, helping customers extend the life of cherished 
family pieces rather than replacing them. In doing so, the building continues a tradition of 
craftsmanship that would have been familiar to earlier generations of Independence

Today, 202 N. Liberty Street remains an active part of the historic Independence Square

helped build and sustain downtown Independence. 
 
Austin Conley Agency - American Family Insurance 
The building at 202–206 N. Liberty Street is one of the most historically significant 
commercial buildings surrounding Independence Square. Today it houses Austin Conley 
American Family Insurance, but its history extends back more than a century and includes 
a direct connection to Harry S. Truman. 
Unlike many Independence Square buildings whose early histories remain difficult to 
document, this structure has a well-established historical record and has been recognized by 
preservation surveys as an important contributing building within the Independence Square 
Historic District. 
Construction and Architecture 
The building was constructed during the late nineteenth or early twentieth century as a 
substantial two-story brick commercial block. Historic architectural surveys describe it as a 
seven-bay commercial structure containing multiple storefronts at street level with office 
space above. 
Notable architectural features include: 
●​ Two-story brick construction

●​ Traditional storefront design 
●​ Decorative brick corbelling 
●​ Stone stringcourses separating floors 
●​ Upper-story office windows overlooking Liberty Street 
●​ A continuous commercial façade typical of Independence Square's business district 
The building remains remarkably intact and still retains much of its historic appearance. 
Early Commercial Occupants 
The earliest documented occupants appear in the 1905 Independence City Directory, 
which places several businesses in the building. 
Known occupants include: 
Year 
Occupant

1905 Charles Capello, clerk 202 N. Liberty 
1905 Jacob Lay, tailor 
204 N. Liberty 
These businesses reflect the diverse mix of merchants and professionals that populated 
Liberty Street during Independence's commercial boom years. 
The Community Savings and Loan Era 
The most important chapter in the building's history began in the 1920s when Community 
Savings and Loan Association occupied the building. 
From approximately 1925 to 1932, the institution operated from 202–206 N. Liberty Street 
and employed Harry S. Truman during a pivotal period in his career. 
Following his service as Presiding Judge of Jackson County, Truman became associated 
with Community Savings and Loan. His office in this building placed him at the center of 
Independence's business community during the years before his election to the United 
States Senate. 
Because of this connection, the building has become one of the lesser-known but important 
Truman-related sites in downtown Independence. 
The Truman Connection 
The significance of the building extends beyond Truman simply working there. 
 

 
 
 
Historic preservation records note that: 
●​ Truman regularly conducted business in the building. 
●​ The structure appears in photographs taken during Truman's lifetime. 
●​ Images of the building appeared in national publications featuring Truman's 
hometown. 
●​ Historic photographs are preserved by the National Archives and the Harry S. 
Truman Presidential Library and Museum. 
When visitors walk past the building today, they are viewing a structure that Truman would 
have entered and exited regularly during an important period of his public and professional 
life. 
Mid-Century and Modern Use 
Like many downtown Independence buildings, 202–206 N. Liberty Street adapted to 
changing commercial needs throughout the twentieth century. 
The building has housed a variety of: 
●​ Professional offices 
●​ Financial services 
●​ Small businesses 
●​ Service-oriented enterprises 
●​ Specialty trade businesses 
Its flexible design allowed it to remain economically viable while many historic downtowns 
across America struggled with suburban competition. 
Current Occupants 
Today, the building continues its tradition of housing professional and service businesses. 
The most visible current tenant is: 
●​ Austin Conley American Family Insurance 
The agency provides: 
●​ Auto insurance 
●​ Home insurance 
●​ Life insurance 
●​ Business insurance 
●​ Financial planning services 
 

 
 
 
This modern use continues the building's long-standing role as a center for professional 
services in downtown Independence.

Historical Significance 
Among the commercial buildings surrounding Independence Square, 202–206 N. Liberty 
Street stands out because of its documented connection to Harry Truman and its 
exceptionally well-preserved commercial architecture. 
The building represents: 
●​ The commercial growth of Independence in the early twentieth century 
●​ The professional business community that shaped downtown Independence 
●​ A documented chapter in Harry Truman's career 
●​ More than a century of continuous commercial use 
Today, it remains one of the most historically significant surviving commercial buildings on 
Liberty Street and an important piece of the story of both Independence Square and Harry 
Truman's hometown.

pdf_content.overview:
Just For You Hair and Nail 
The building at 208 N. Liberty Street is a historic commercial storefront located just north of 
Independence Square. Today it is occupied by Just For You Hair & Nail, a locally owned 
beauty salon that continues a long tradition of service-oriented businesses operating in 
downtown Independence. 
The Building and Its Historic Setting 
208 N. Liberty Street is part of a continuous row of commercial buildings that developed 
around Independence Square during the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. 
Liberty Street served as one of the principal commercial corridors feeding into the 
courthouse square, making it a desirable location for merchants, professionals, and service 
businesses. 
The building shares many characteristics common to historic Square-area storefronts: 
●​ Brick commercial construction 
●​ Ground-floor retail or service space 
●​ Large storefront display windows 
●​ Shared walls with neighboring buildings 
●​ Flexible interiors that could accommodate changing businesses 
Its design allowed the building to remain commercially useful as downtown Independence 
evolved through successive generations. 
Early Twentieth-Century Context 
By the early 1900s, North Liberty Street was home to a variety of: 
●​ Retail merchants 
●​ Tailors 
●​ Insurance offices 
●​ Financial institutions 
●​ Professional offices 
●​ Personal service businesses 
The building stands immediately north of the historic 202–206 N. Liberty Street 
commercial block, one of the best-documented buildings in the district and the former 
location of Community Savings and Loan Association. 
Connection to Harry Truman's Independence 
 

 
 
 
Although no direct evidence has surfaced linking Harry Truman to 208 N. Liberty Street itself, 
the building occupied the same commercial district that Truman knew throughout his life. 
Nearby, Harry S. Truman worked for Community Savings and Loan between approximately 
1925 and 1932. During Truman's years as a businessman, Jackson County official, U.S. 
Senator, Vice President, and President, Liberty Street remained part of the downtown 
Independence environment he frequently visited. 
As a result, the building stands within the historic Truman-era streetscape that survives 
around Independence Square today. 
Documented Modern Occupancy 
The most clearly documented occupant is Just For You Hair & Nail, which has operated as a 
beauty salon providing services such as: 
●​ Haircuts and styling 
●​ Hair coloring 
●​ Nail services 
●​ Personal beauty treatments 
The business continues the long-standing tradition of locally owned service establishments 
serving residents of Independence and the surrounding area. 
Building Evolution 
Like many Independence Square buildings, 208 N. Liberty Street has adapted to changing 
economic and commercial needs over time. 
The structure has survived: 
●​ The transition from horse-drawn transportation to automobiles 
●​ The Great Depression 
●​ World War II 
●​ The suburban shopping era 
●​ Urban renewal pressures 
●​ Modern downtown revitalization efforts 
Its continued commercial use has helped preserve both the building and the historic 
character of North Liberty Street.

generations of downtown commerce. 
The Up Dog Era 
The most documented chapter in the building's recent history began when Up Dog opened 
around 2011. The restaurant transformed the storefront into a nostalgic diner-style space

beverages.

downtown district. Its significance comes from more than a century of continuous 
commercial use and its role in preserving the historic streetscape of Independence Square. 
Today, through Up Dog, the building continues the purpose for which it was originally built: 
serving the public, supporting local business, and contributing to the vitality of downtown 
Independence.

pdf_content.overview:
Square Pizza 
The building at 208 W. Maple Avenue is a historic commercial storefront on the west side of 
Independence Square and is currently occupied by Square Pizza. The building is part of the 
continuous row of late nineteenth- and early twentieth-century commercial structures that 
define the character of downtown Independence. 
For generations, the west side of Maple Avenue was occupied by: 
●​ Retail stores 
●​ Restaurants 
●​ Professional offices 
●​ Service businesses 
●​ Specialty merchants 
The building sits among several long-established Square businesses, including Dave's 
Bakery & Deli at 214 W. Maple Avenue and nearby entertainment and retail establishments 
that have helped keep the Square active through multiple generations. 
The Square Pizza Era 
The most documented chapter of the building's history began when Square Pizza 
established its restaurant at 208 W. Maple Avenue. 
The restaurant became known for: 
●​ Square pan-style pizzas 
●​ Retro diner-inspired décor 
●​ Arcade games 
●​ Specialty pizzas named after local themes and landmarks 
●​ Family-friendly dining 
 

 
 
 
The restaurant markets itself as "The Only Square Pizza in Missouri" and has become a 
recognizable destination on Independence Square. Square Pizza has been operating from 
2011 - Present. 
Historical Significance 
The significance of 208 W. Maple Avenue comes from its continuity as a commercial 
storefront within the historic core of Independence. 
For more than a century, the building has served the same essential purpose: providing 
space for local business and community interaction. Today, through Square Pizza, it 
continues that tradition while helping preserve the historic character and economic vitality of 
Independence Square.
